resume:
STAFF LEARNING DESIGNER & CURRICULUM SYSTEMS LEAD
Philadelphia, PA | 13 years in K-12 and higher-ed instructional design

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
I am a staff-level learning experience designer who builds curriculum at the program and system level rather than the single-course level. Over thirteen years I have moved from classroom teaching, to instructional design for a single university department, to leading the design standards and tooling that dozens of designers and faculty now build against. My focus is on measurable learning outcomes, accessibility, and the unglamorous infrastructure work that makes a large catalog of courses consistent, maintainable, and evidence-based. I care about whether students actually learn, not whether a course looks polished, and I have spent a lot of my career trying to make that distinction legible to leadership through assessment data, learning analytics, and controlled pilots. I work most effectively in the seam between faculty subject-matter experts, who own the content, and the platform and data teams, who own the delivery. A large part of my job is translating between those groups and removing the friction that causes good curriculum to decay over time.

CORE AREAS
Backward design and outcomes mapping; competency-based and mastery-based program design; formative and summative assessment design; rubric and item-bank development; Universal Design for Learning and WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility; learning analytics and A/B-style instructional experiments; LMS administration and migration (Canvas, Brightspace); authoring-tool governance; faculty enablement and train-the-trainer programs; accreditation evidence and program review.

EXPERIENCE

Keystone University Online — Staff Learning Designer, Curriculum Systems (2019 to present)
I lead the design-standards function for an online division that serves roughly 14,000 students across 60-plus programs. When I joined, every program built courses its own way, quality varied wildly between sections of the same course, and there was no shared definition of what a 'complete' course module even looked like. I led the creation of a course-design rubric and a reusable module template, then ran the multi-semester effort to bring the existing catalog into alignment. Rather than mandating the standard top-down, I piloted it with three volunteer programs, instrumented the courses to compare withdrawal rates and assessment-completion rates before and after, and used those results to win faculty senate buy-in before any wider rollout. I also rebuilt our assessment infrastructure: I introduced a governed item bank so that high-stakes questions were reviewed, tagged to outcomes, and rotated, which reduced the academic-integrity incidents that had been climbing each term. On the tooling side, I owned the evaluation and eventual migration of our authoring workflow, and I built the faculty enablement program — office hours, a self-serve pattern library, and a cohort-based onboarding for new designers — that let us scale design support without simply hiring more designers. I partner constantly with the institutional-research team to make sure the things we claim about learning outcomes are actually defensible, because that evidence is what we hand to accreditors.

Selected outcomes at Keystone:
- Brought 48 of 60-plus programs onto the shared module template over four semesters; section-to-section variance in student-reported clarity dropped measurably in our end-of-course surveys.
- The three-program pilot showed a reduction in course-withdrawal rate from roughly 12 percent to roughly 8 percent in the redesigned courses, which is the result I leaned on to get the wider mandate approved.
- Stood up a governed item bank covering the twelve highest-enrollment courses, cutting flagged integrity incidents in those courses by more than half year over year.
- Reduced new-course build time from an average of about sixteen weeks to about nine weeks by giving designers a starting template and a pattern library instead of a blank page.

Lighthouse Charter Network — Senior Instructional Designer (2015 to 2019)
I designed competency-based middle-school curriculum across a five-school charter network and supported the teachers delivering it. My signature project was converting the network's math sequence from a time-based model to a mastery-based one, where students advanced on demonstrated proficiency rather than seat time. I built the proficiency scales, the formative checks, and the teacher-facing dashboards that made student mastery visible, and I ran the professional-development series that retrained teachers on how to use class time differently. This was as much a change-management project as a design project; many veteran teachers were skeptical, and a real part of the work was sitting in classrooms, co-teaching, and earning credibility before the model stuck. I also owned the network's accessibility remediation, bringing instructional materials into WCAG compliance after an OCR complaint.

Selected outcomes at Lighthouse:
- Mastery-based math pilot moved end-of-year proficiency on the state assessment up by a meaningful margin in the two pilot schools relative to the control schools, though I would want to caveat the confounds before claiming causation.
- Built and delivered a 30-hour teacher PD series that every math teacher in the network completed.

Greenfield Public Schools — Classroom Teacher, Middle-School Mathematics (2011 to 2015)
Taught 7th and 8th grade mathematics. Served on the district curriculum committee in my final two years, which is where I first started designing assessments and pacing guides for teachers beyond my own classroom and discovered that I cared more about the design layer than about my own roster.

EDUCATION
M.Ed., Learning Design and Technology, Commonwealth University, 2014.
B.S., Mathematics, State College, 2011.

CERTIFICATIONS & SKILLS
Quality Matters certified peer reviewer; WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility; Canvas and Brightspace administration; rubric and item-bank design; learning analytics; SQL for outcomes reporting; basic Python for data cleaning; survey and experiment design.

SELECTED ARTIFACTS
Course-design rubric and module template (Keystone, used division-wide); mastery-based math proficiency scales (Lighthouse); faculty enablement pattern library; accessibility remediation playbook.
jd: We are seeking a Staff Learning Designer to own curriculum-design standards and assessment infrastructure for a large online university division. This is a systems role, not a single-course role: you will define the design standards that dozens of faculty and designers build against, own the assessment item-bank governance, and lead the evidence work that supports program review and accreditation. You will be expected to drive adoption of standards across politically independent academic departments without formal authority over them, which means your influence must come from credibility and evidence rather than mandate. The strongest candidates can describe, with specificity, a time they changed how a skeptical group of faculty worked — including what the resistance actually was, the specific intervention they made, and how they measured whether learning outcomes (not just satisfaction) improved. You will partner with institutional research and the LMS platform team. Experience with competency-based design, accessibility compliance, and learning analytics is required.

history.q: Your resume centers on systems-level design. Tell me about the course-design rubric rollout at Keystone — what problem were you actually solving?
You ran a three-program pilot before the wider mandate. Why pilot first instead of just rolling the standard out?

The JD is blunt that this role has no formal authority over departments. Tell me about a time you changed how a skeptical group of faculty worked — what was the resistance, and what did you specifically do?
And how did you know learning outcomes improved — not just that satisfaction went up?

candidate_last_answer: Right, so — okay, this is a good question, and there are kind of a few angles to it. So at Keystone, and actually also a bit at Lighthouse, the way I think about outcomes versus satisfaction is, you know, satisfaction is easy to move and it doesn't always mean anything, which everybody sort of knows but doesn't always act on, and — sorry, let me back up. The faculty were skeptical, that's the thing, and one professor in particular was really vocal, and I remember thinking, you know, I need to win him over, and we did a lot of, um, office hours and co-design, and there's a whole culture piece there about respecting that they own the content... and I guess what I'm trying to say is we looked at the data, we definitely looked at the data, the withdrawal rates and the assessment stuff, and it was better. I had a number for that somewhere. Does that get at what you're asking, or did you want the satisfaction part specifically?

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: we looked at the data, we definitely looked at the data, the withdrawal rates and the assessment stuff, and it was better. I had a number for that somewhere

expected-yield: the specific learning-outcome metric (e.g. assessment-completion or proficiency, not just withdrawal rate), the before/after values, and how it was attributed to the redesign rather than to confounds — cutting through the rambling to one defensible figure
